did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for melanoma classification and applied five visual explanation techniques (Grad-CAM, Grad-CAM++, Score-CAM, Eigen-CAM, and LIME) to analyze agreement with dermatologists on clinical features (asymmetry, border irregularity, color heterogeneity) in 100 melanoma images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as 'TYPE Original Research' and presents original experimental results, including a Methods section describing the testing of five visual explanation techniques (Grad-CAM, Grad-CAM++, Score-CAM, Eigen-CAM, and LIME) on 100 melanoma images, and a Results section reporting specific agreement rates and rankings derived from this experiment.

title: Explainability agreement between dermatologists and five visual explanations techniques in deep neural networks for melanoma AI classification
brief_summary: This study evaluated the agreement between five visual explanation techniques (Grad-CAM, Grad-CAM++, Score-CAM, Eigen-CAM, and LIME) and dermatologists regarding clinical features of melanoma. The researchers analyzed 100 melanoma images using a deep convolutional neural network trained on public datasets including HAM10000, MSK, BCN20000, and Derm7pt. Two dermatologists scored the visual explanation maps against the ABC rule criteria of asymmetry, border irregularity, and color heterogeneity using a semi-quantitative scale. The results showed that Grad-CAM achieved the highest total and partial agreement rate at 93.6%, followed by LIME at 89.8%. Dermatologists ranked Grad-CAM and Grad-CAM++ as their preferred techniques, demonstrating significant agreement between human diagnostic criteria and AI explainability methods.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focused on 100 melanoma images selected from public datasets (HAM10000, MSK, BCN20000, and Derm7pt). The CNN model was trained on these datasets with two classes: melanoma (18%) and non-melanoma (82), but the explainability analysis was performed specifically on the melanoma images.
Defined as the second class in the dataset composition (82%) used to train the CNN model, alongside melanoma (18%). Not the primary focus of the visual explanation analysis.

all_task_types.details: The paper evaluates a deep convolutional neural network (CNN) trained for binary classification of melanoma (18%) versus non-melanoma (82%) using datasets HAM10000, MSK, BCN20000, and Derm7pt. The model uses a sigmoid function for prediction and was evaluated on 100 melanoma images using visual explanation techniques (Grad-CAM, Grad-CAM++, Score-CAM, Eigen-CAM, LIME) to analyze agreement with dermatologists on clinical features.

datasets_used.name: Melanoma Image Cohort for Explainability Analysis
datasets_used.description: A subset of 100 melanoma images selected from the public datasets (HAM10000, MSK, BCN20000, and Derm7pt) specifically for the evaluation of five visual explanation techniques (Grad-CAM, Grad-CAM++, Score-CAM, Eigen-CAM, and LIME) and agreement with dermatologists.

use.testing: Used for evaluating the agreement between five visual explanation techniques and dermatologists on clinical features (asymmetry, border irregularity, color heterogeneity).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess inter-rater reliability and agreement rates (total, partial, and no agreement) between dermatologists and visual explanation techniques for clinical features (asymmetry, border, color).
To grade the alignment between visual explanation maps and clinical criteria (ABC rule) on a scale of 0-2 for asymmetry, border, and color heterogeneity.
To determine the consistency between the two dermatologists' assessments (60% total and 40% partial agreement).
To rank the five visual explanation techniques based on dermatologists' preferences.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported agreement rates (total, partial, no agreement) and rankings based on semi-quantitative scoring and confusion matrix analysis, but did not perform statistical significance testing between the techniques.

Melanoma Image Cohort for Explainability.description: 100 melanoma images selected from HAM10000, MSK, BCN20000, and Derm7pt datasets, evaluated for agreement with dermatologists on ABC rule criteria (asymmetry, border, color) using five visual explanation techniques.
Melanoma Image Cohort for Explainability.notes: Grad-CAM achieved the highest total+partial agreement rate (93.6%). Dermatologists ranked Grad-CAM and Grad-CAM++ as their preferred techniques. Inter-expert agreement between the two dermatologists was 60% total and 40% partial.

CNN Model Performance (Training/Testing.description: Performance of the deep convolutional neural network trained on the combined dataset (HAM10000, MSK, BCN20000, Derm7pt) for melanoma vs. non-melanoma classification.
CNN Model Performance (Training/Testing.notes: Model trained on 26,342 images (18% melanoma, 82% non-melanoma). Data augmentation included rotation, shear, horizontal flip, and zoom. Images resized to 220x220.

conclusions: Grad-CAM achieved the highest total and partial agreement rate (93.6%) with dermatologists regarding clinical features of melanoma, followed by LIME (89.8%), Grad-CAM++ (88.0%), Eigen-CAM (86.4%), and Score-CAM (84.6%).

Dermatologists ranked Grad-CAM and Grad-CAM++ as their preferred visual explanation techniques, indicating a preference for methods that highlight specific, clinically relevant areas over those that delimit the entire lesion or create large rectangular maps.

There is a significant agreement between the clinical features used by dermatologists to diagnose melanomas (asymmetry, border irregularity, and color heterogeneity) and the patterns highlighted by visual explanation techniques, particularly Grad-CAM.

Grad-CAM was identified as the most reliable technique for identifying border abnormalities and color heterogeneity, while Grad-CAM++ showed the best performance for asymmetry detection with only 3% no agreement.
Eigen-CAM and Score-CAM performed poorly in qualitative assessments; Eigen-CAM was criticized for creating indiscriminate rectangular maps, and Score-CAM for poor delimitation of the lesion.

The evaluation of explainability with human experts is ideal for assessing the understanding and applicability of AI methods in clinical contexts, as it helps bridge the gap between black-box AI predictions and clinical diagnostic criteria.

The study highlights that while saliency maps are located on lesions, they do not automatically justify clinically meaningful correlations, and further research is needed to objectively explore subjective matters and minimize model and expert biases in clinical AI adoption.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a small, specific cohort of 100 melanoma images for the explainability analysis, which may limit the generalizability of the agreement rates.
The evaluation relies on subjective semi-quantitative scoring by only two dermatologists, introducing potential expert bias and limiting statistical robustness.
No formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals were reported for the agreement rates or rankings.

The CNN model was trained on a combined dataset with significant class imbalance (18% melanoma, 82% non-melanoma), addressed via focal loss and class weighting, but performance metrics (ACC 0.81, AUC 0.94) are reported without external validation details.
The study acknowledges that saliency maps located on lesions do not automatically justify clinically meaningful correlations, highlighting a gap between visual explanation and clinical justification.
The research was conducted at a single institution (Hospital Israelita Albert Einstein, Brazil), limiting external validation across diverse populations or imaging devices.
The authors note that no standard method existed for this specific type of subjective evaluation, leading to a custom study design that may not be easily replicable.
Inter-expert agreement between the two dermatologists was moderate (60% total, 40% partial), suggesting variability in human interpretation of the visual maps.
